title: ERRO[0000] Cannot get exit code: failed to get journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When executing basic `podman run` command, I keep getting this error message:
```
ERRO[0000] Cannot get exit code: failed to get journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address 
```
I've been unable to find a trouble shooting guide for this issue.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman run --rm -ti alpine /bin/true; echo $?`

**Describe the results you received:**

```
ERRO[0000] Cannot get exit code: failed to get journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address 
127
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

No error, and return code = 0

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

It used to happen every other run, now that I've upgraded several SW packages to their latest version, it always occurs.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.1
API Version:  3.3.1
Go Version:   go1.16.7
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
Listing... Done
podman/testing,unstable,now 3.3.1+ds2-1 amd64 [installed]
podman/stable 3.0.1+dfsg1-3+b2 amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**

Yes

It seems to be the latest version, and the trouble shooting guide does not seem to give any advice on this specific error.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

* Physical: AMD Ryzen3 5950x, 32GB ram, single SSD, no network mount
* Debian 11, with default kernel (5.10) and with experimental kernel (5.14), using crun
* Docker installed (and running ok) docker.io/stable,testing,unstable,now 20.10.5+dfsg1-1+b5 amd64 [installed] 

Please note that when replacing `crun` with `runc` I get a totally different error

```
Error: container_linux.go:367: starting container process caused: error adding seccomp filter rule for syscall bdflush: permission denied: OCI permission denied
```
for which the recommended workaround seems to be to use ... `crun` :-)

comments.body: You need to change your `eventLogger` to `file` in the containers.conf config file.
thanks a lot @Luap99, it seems to make the job.
Starting with version 3.4 we should be able to fall back to `file` automatically when we cannot use journald.

@Luap99 I'm still seeing the issue on Podman 3.4.4 shipped with Ubuntu 22.04
``` console
$ podman version
Version:      3.4.4
API Version:  3.4.4
Go Version:   go1.17.3
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

I'm able to workaround the issue by setting `events_logger = "file"` in containers.conf.
This version is no longer supported by us upstream, please try with at least v4.2 and see if this fixes your problem. If not file a new bug with a reproducer.

I am seeing this with podman 4.3.1. This error just started today, it's quite odd.
I am running podman in rootless mode with a dedicated user.

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.1
API Version:  4.3.1
Go Version:   go1.19.3
Git Commit:   814b7b003cc630bf6ab188274706c383f9fb9915-dirty
Built:        Sun Nov 20 23:32:45 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64

Linux 6.1.2-arch1-1 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Sat, 31 Dec 2022 17:40:35 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

events_logger in /etc/containers/containers.conf was commented out, but by default was logging to journald. 

I have edited /etc/containers/containers.conf to use events_logger="file".
Additionally, I set events_logfile_path= to use a path stored on local system storage, instead of using RAM-backed storage like /run ([referencing this comment](https://opendev.org/openstack/tripleo-ansible/commit/79be78bba35199c5b26632e51d8bda411a8239c5) elsewhere as an example of possible OOM scenario, thus the default/change to journald)

I have also fully updated my system, as well as doing a full reboot.
The issue seems resolved for now, but I felt it should be noted this is happening with a version of podman based on v4.2+.
If the issue reappears, I will open a new bug report as mentioned by Luap99.


Other errors that appear are regarding the cni-podman ports entering a disabled state, unsure if related or just side-effects.

```
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth6axxxx) entered disabled state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] device veth6xxxx left promiscuous mode
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth6axxxx) entered disabled state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered blocking state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered disabled state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] device veth8xxxx entered promiscuous mode
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered blocking state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered forwarding state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:23 2023] IPv6: ADDRCONF(NETDEV_CHANGE): veth8axxxx: link becomes ready
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:26 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered disabled state
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:26 2023] device veth8axxxx left promiscuous mode
[Wed Jan  4 15:32:26 2023] cni-podman9: port 1(veth8axxxx) entered disabled state
```
